course_info.title: Crisis Communication in Digital Age
prerequisites: DC201 - Social Media Strategy and Management

learning_objectives: Define crisis communication and classify crises by type, severity, and origin
Explain how digital and social media have accelerated crisis timelines from days to minutes
Analyze real crisis case studies to identify decision points where outcomes diverged
readings: Harvard Business Review, 'The Best and Worst Corporate Apologies of the Last Decade'

lecture_notes.introduction: Digital channels have compressed the crisis timeline from days to minutes. A single tweet, screenshot, or video can escalate from local embarrassment to global news cycle before your team finishes their morning coffee. This course prepares you to respond effectively when speed and judgment must coexist.
key_concepts: Crisis types: operational failure, reputational attack, executive misconduct, data breach, social media backlash, product recall
The viral escalation cycle: trigger event, amplification, mainstream media pickup, public demand for response
The golden hour: the first 60 minutes of a crisis determine the trajectory more than anything that follows
Stakeholder mapping: employees, customers, media, regulators, investors, and the public each require different messages
Crisis vs. issue: distinguishing genuine crises from manageable issues that do not warrant crisis-level response
lecture_notes.demonstration: Minute-by-minute reconstruction of the United Airlines passenger removal crisis, identifying every decision point, communication failure, and missed opportunity for containment
setup_requirements: Crisis case study archive (provided on LMS)

review.value: The first 60 minutes after a crisis becomes public; the initial response sets the narrative frame, and silence during this window cedes control to critics, media, and speculation

Trigger event occurs, early social media users amplify, engagement triggers algorithmic distribution, mainstream media picks up the story, public pressure demands organizational response; each stage narrows available response options

learning_objectives: Develop a crisis communication plan with roles, protocols, and pre-approved templates
Conduct a vulnerability audit to identify potential crises before they occur
Design a crisis monitoring system using social listening and alert tools

lecture_notes.introduction: The worst time to write a crisis plan is during a crisis. Preparedness separates organizations that navigate crises successfully from those that are destroyed by them. A crisis plan does not predict every scenario, but it ensures your team knows their roles, has authority to act, and can communicate within minutes.
key_concepts: Crisis communication plan components: crisis team roster, escalation matrix, stakeholder contact lists, holding statements, channel protocols
Vulnerability audit: mapping potential crises by probability and severity to prioritize preparation
Dark site and holding statements: pre-approved content that can be deployed within minutes
Social listening for early warning: monitoring brand mentions, sentiment shifts, and trending keywords
Crisis simulation exercises: tabletop drills that test the plan before a real crisis demands it
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a crisis communication plan skeleton for a university: defining the crisis team, creating an escalation matrix, drafting three holding statements, and setting up social listening alerts for early warning

review.value: A pre-approved, generic statement acknowledging that the organization is aware of a situation and gathering information, designed to be deployable within minutes while buying time for a more substantive response
A systematic assessment of potential crisis scenarios ranked by probability and severity, conducted before any crisis occurs, enabling targeted preparation for the highest-risk scenarios

learning_objectives: Craft crisis messages that address stakeholder concerns with appropriate empathy and accountability
Apply the SCCT (Situational Crisis Communication Theory) to select response strategies
Manage multi-channel communication during active crisis situations
readings: Case studies: Johnson & Johnson Tylenol (1982), BP Deepwater Horizon (2010)

lecture_notes.introduction: What you say during a crisis matters less than when you say it and whether your actions match your words. The best crisis messages are prompt, transparent, empathetic, and paired with visible action. The worst are late, defensive, blame-shifting, and disconnected from reality.
key_concepts: SCCT response strategies: deny, diminish, rebuild (apology, compensation, corrective action), bolster
The apology framework: acknowledge, take responsibility, express empathy, state corrective actions, provide timeline
Channel strategy: which messages go where (press release, social post, email, website, internal)
Spokesperson preparation: message discipline, bridging techniques, and avoiding common traps
Internal communication: employees learn about the crisis from you first, or they learn from Twitter
lecture_notes.demonstration: Live crisis simulation: a crisis scenario unfolds in real-time over 45 minutes, and students must draft responses for social media, press release, employee email, and customer notification, making decisions under time pressure

review.value: Situational Crisis Communication Theory: matches response strategies to crisis types based on organizational responsibility. High responsibility (product defect) requires rebuild strategies (apology, compensation). Low responsibility (natural disaster) allows diminish or bolster strategies.

Employees must receive crisis information before or simultaneously with the public; employees who learn about a crisis from social media or news feel betrayed and become unreliable brand ambassadors during the crisis

learning_objectives: Manage social media channels during an active crisis including response protocols and content pausing
Handle misinformation, trolls, and coordinated attacks during crises
Use social media monitoring to track crisis sentiment and adjust messaging in real time
readings: Paine, Measure What Matters, Ch. 11 - Crisis Measurement
Selected social media crisis case studies (provided on LMS)

lecture_notes.introduction: Social media is simultaneously the most dangerous accelerant and the most powerful tool during a crisis. It amplifies bad news instantly but also gives you a direct channel to every stakeholder without media gatekeepers. Mastering social media crisis management is the most time-sensitive skill in modern communications.
key_concepts: Content pause protocol: immediately halting all scheduled content to avoid tone-deaf posts during a crisis
Response triage: which comments to respond to publicly, which to take to DM, and which to ignore
Misinformation management: correcting false narratives with facts without amplifying them
Troll vs. concerned stakeholder: distinguishing bad-faith attacks from legitimate criticism
Real-time sentiment tracking: using social listening to monitor whether your response is working
lecture_notes.demonstration: Social media crisis simulation: students manage a mock brand's social media accounts during a live crisis, deciding which posts to pause, which comments to respond to, and how to communicate updates in real time

review.value: The immediate suspension of all pre-scheduled social media content when a crisis emerges; a promotional post publishing during a crisis appears tone-deaf and amplifies negative sentiment
Attempting to suppress or aggressively counter negative information often amplifies it; sometimes the best social media response is a measured acknowledgment rather than a combative correction

learning_objectives: Develop a post-crisis recovery plan that rebuilds stakeholder trust over time
Conduct a post-crisis audit to identify lessons learned and plan improvements
Apply reputation repair strategies through sustained communications efforts
readings: Reputation Institute, 'Rebuilding Trust After a Crisis'

lecture_notes.introduction: The crisis is over when the news cycle moves on, but reputation repair takes months or years. Organizations that learn from crises and demonstrate genuine change rebuild stronger than before. Those that resume business as usual without addressing root causes face repeated crises.
key_concepts: Post-crisis audit: what happened, what worked, what failed, and what must change
Reputation repair strategies: corrective action, organizational reform, sustained positive communication
Trust rebuilding timeline: research shows trust recovery takes 3-5 times longer than trust destruction
Stakeholder re-engagement: tailored outreach to each affected group with specific actions taken
Crisis plan revision: incorporating lessons learned into updated preparedness plans
lecture_notes.demonstration: Developing a 90-day reputation recovery plan for an organization that experienced a data breach, including stakeholder outreach, communication campaign, and brand health monitoring

review.value: Trust is built slowly through consistent positive actions but destroyed quickly by a single negative event; recovery requires sustained effort over a timeline 3-5 times longer than the destructive event
The most effective reputation repair strategy: clearly communicating the specific changes made to prevent recurrence, with verifiable commitments and progress updates

questions.question: Explain the golden hour concept in crisis communication and describe three specific actions a communications team should take within the first 60 minutes of a crisis becoming public.
questions.answer: The golden hour is the first 60 minutes after a crisis becomes public, during which the initial response sets the narrative frame and determines whether the organization or its critics control the story. Three actions: (1) Activate the crisis team and confirm roles and communication channels. (2) Deploy a holding statement across social media and the website acknowledging awareness of the situation and promising more information. (3) Pause all scheduled content and advertising to prevent tone-deaf automated posts from publishing.

questions.question: What is a holding statement, and why must it be pre-approved rather than drafted during the crisis?
questions.answer: A holding statement is a brief, generic statement acknowledging that the organization is aware of a developing situation, takes it seriously, and is actively gathering information. It must be pre-approved because during a crisis, decision-makers are under extreme time pressure and stress. Drafting, reviewing, and approving a statement from scratch can take hours when the golden hour demands a response in minutes. Pre-approved templates only need situation-specific details filled in, enabling deployment within 15-30 minutes.

rubric.key: Explains pre-approval rationale with time pressure

quizzes.topic: Crisis Response and Social Media Management

questions.question: Describe the SCCT (Situational Crisis Communication Theory) and explain which response strategy is appropriate when an organization is clearly at fault for a product safety defect.
questions.answer: SCCT matches crisis response strategies to crisis types based on the level of organizational responsibility. The four strategy clusters are: deny (attack accuser, denial, scapegoating), diminish (excuse, justification), rebuild (compensation, apology), and bolster (reminder of past good work, victimization, ingratiation). For a product safety defect, the organization has high responsibility, making rebuild strategies appropriate: a full apology accepting responsibility, compensation for affected customers, and corrective action (recall, redesign, new quality controls). Deny or diminish strategies would be perceived as dishonest and would amplify reputational damage.

questions.question: Explain the Streisand Effect and describe a scenario where an organization's attempt to suppress negative information on social media would backfire.
questions.answer: The Streisand Effect describes the phenomenon where attempting to hide, remove, or censor information draws far more attention to it than ignoring it would have. Scenario: A restaurant receives a negative review on Twitter with a photo of a food safety issue. Instead of acknowledging and addressing the concern, the restaurant threatens the reviewer with legal action and reports the post for removal. Other users notice the threat, screenshot the original post, and share it widely with commentary about the restaurant trying to silence legitimate criticism. The story goes viral, reaching millions instead of the original poster's few hundred followers, generating news coverage about the threat and the food safety issue simultaneously.

major_assignments.title: Crisis Communication Plan and Simulation
major_assignments.description: Develop a comprehensive crisis communication plan for a real organization, then execute a crisis simulation demonstrating the plan in action. The plan must include vulnerability audit, crisis team structure, response protocols, message templates, and monitoring systems.
objectives: Conduct a vulnerability audit identifying the organization's top crisis risks
Build a crisis communication plan with roles, escalation matrix, and pre-approved messages
Execute a simulated crisis demonstrating rapid, multi-channel response
Conduct a post-simulation debrief analyzing performance and identifying improvements
deliverables: Crisis communication plan (12-15 pages) with all components
Vulnerability audit with risk matrix (probability vs. severity)
Simulation response package: all messages produced during the exercise with timestamps
Post-simulation debrief report (3-4 pages) with performance analysis and plan revisions

criteria: Vulnerability audit identifies genuine, specific risks for the organization
Crisis team roles and escalation matrix are clear and actionable
Holding statements and templates are well-crafted and adaptable
Monitoring and alert systems are appropriate and realistic
Response timing meets golden hour benchmarks
Messages are appropriate for each channel and stakeholder group
Decisions under pressure demonstrate sound judgment
Multi-channel coordination is effective and consistent
Performance analysis is honest and specific
Lessons learned are actionable and grounded in simulation evidence
Debrief demonstrates understanding of crisis communication principles
Comprehensive crisis plan demonstrating thorough preparation
Effective simulation performance under time pressure
Professional deliverables suitable for organizational adoption

quizzes.description: Regular quizzes on crisis communication theory and case studies

crisis_simulations.description: In-class crisis simulations with real-time response evaluation

major_assignment.description: Comprehensive crisis communication plan and simulation exercise

midterm_examination.description: Case study analysis examination on crisis response strategies

final_examination.description: Live crisis simulation examination with comprehensive response evaluation

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all sessions, especially crisis simulations which cannot be replicated. Missing a simulation results in zero credit for that exercise.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late assignments lose 10% per day. Crisis plan deliverables cannot be accepted more than 3 days late since simulation exercises depend on completed plans.
course_policies.collaboration: Crisis simulations may involve team roles. All written deliverables are individual. Sharing crisis plans between students is prohibited as each targets a different organization.
course_policies.academic_integrity: Case study analysis must cite sources. Crisis plans must be original work. Copying crisis plans from online templates without substantial customization constitutes academic dishonesty.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need access to social media platforms for monitoring, a laptop with internet access, and notification tools for real-time simulation participation.
